01_initial_request.target_name: Distributed Secondary Control for Power Allocation and Voltage Restoration in Islanded DC Microgrids
01_initial_request.input_data: Islanded DC microgrid with multiple distributed generators (DGs) connected in parallel; droop control parameters (gains k_i, line resistances R_i); nominal DC voltage V*; communication graph among DG controllers; DC bus voltage measurement available to at least one DG.
01_initial_request.user_intent: To overcome the fundamental trade-off between power sharing accuracy and voltage regulation in islanded DC microgrids. While droop control enables decentralized power sharing, it causes DC bus voltage deviation. Centralized secondary control suffers from single-point failures and cannot meet plug-and-play requirements. A distributed solution is needed that uses only limited local information and DC bus voltage feedback.
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: 1) Restore DC bus voltage V_b to nominal value V*; 2) Maintain proportional power sharing accuracy I_i/I_j = (R_j + k_j)/(R_i + k_i); 3) Ensure all secondary control inputs converge to the same steady-state value u_i^s = u_j^s; 4) Achieve full distributed implementation with only neighbor communication; 5) Simplify using pinning control so only one DG needs DC bus voltage feedback.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: In islanded DC microgrids, droop control V_ref_i = V* - k_i * I_i achieves proportional power sharing I_i/I_j ≈ k_j/k_i when k_i >> R_i, but causes bus voltage deviation V_b = V* - (R_i + k_i)I_i. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: Existing distributed secondary control methods use feed-forward compensation requiring global average current/voltage values, which are difficult to obtain in a distributed manner. A feedback-based approach is needed. [Decision] To address the voltage deviation while maintaining power sharing, chose to formulate the secondary control problem with a key constraint: all secondary control inputs u_i must be equal at steady state (u_i^s = u_j^s) to preserve the power sharing property established by droop control.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: The key insight is that to maintain power sharing accuracy while restoring voltage, all secondary control inputs must converge to the same value at steady state. This constraint transforms the problem into a consensus-based control design with voltage restoration as the tracking objective.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The constraint that all u_i must be equal at steady state has been identified as essential for maintaining power sharing. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: A controller must be designed that simultaneously achieves voltage restoration and consensus among u_i, using only local and neighbor information. [Decision] To solve this dual-objective problem, designed a distributed PI controller that combines voltage restoration error and control input consensus error: e_i = c_i(V* - V_b) + Σ_{j∈N_i}a_{ij}(u_i - u_j), where c_i is the pinning gain (nonzero only for DGs with bus voltage access). The PI controller: u_i = K_P * e_i + K_I * ∫e_i dt.

tool.name: Distributed PI controller with pinning control

parameters.error_signal: e_i = c_i(V* - V_b) + Σ_{j∈N_i} a_{ij}(u_i - u_j)
parameters.pinning_gain: c_i > 0 only for DGs with bus voltage access, c_i = 0 otherwise
parameters.communication: Each secondary controller exchanges u_i with neighbors only

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The designed controller elegantly combines two objectives in one error signal: the pinning term c_i(V* - V_b) drives voltage restoration, while the consensus term Σa_{ij}(u_i - u_j) ensures all control inputs converge. With pinning control, only one DG needs DC bus voltage measurement, significantly simplifying the system architecture.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The distributed PI controller with pinning control has been designed. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The stability of the closed-loop system needs to be rigorously analyzed, and sufficient conditions for controller parameters (K_P, K_I) must be established. [Decision] To analyze stability, derived the closed-loop system dynamics and used Lyapunov stability theory to establish sufficient conditions for the controller parameters. The analysis involves the properties of the graph Laplacian matrix and the pinning gain matrix.

parameters.closed_loop_system: Coupled system of bus voltage dynamics, secondary control inputs, and consensus dynamics
parameters.lyapunov_function: V = e^T P e where e is the combined error vector
parameters.stability_condition: Sufficient condition on K_P, K_I involving eigenvalues of the Laplacian and pinning matrices

02_agent_trajectory.observation: A sufficient condition for stability is established: the controller gains K_P and K_I must satisfy conditions involving the second smallest eigenvalue of the Laplacian matrix (algebraic connectivity) and the pinning gains. This provides practical guidelines for parameter tuning. The analysis proves that the proposed distributed controller achieves both voltage restoration and power sharing simultaneously.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: Theoretical stability guarantees have been established with sufficient conditions for controller parameters. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The proposed method needs to be validated through numerical simulation on a realistic islanded DC microgrid test system. [Decision] To validate the method, built an islanded DC microgrid test system in MATLAB Simulink with 4 DGs and conducted simulation studies for voltage restoration and power sharing under varying load conditions, with both full distributed control and simplified pinning control.

parameters.test_system: 4 DGs connected in parallel to DC bus
parameters.simulation_scenarios: Load step changes, varying DG parameters, comparison with centralized control
parameters.pinning_control_test: Only DG #1 has bus voltage feedback (c_1 > 0, c_2 = c_3 = c_4 = 0)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Simulation results confirm: 1) DC bus voltage is restored to nominal value after load changes; 2) Power sharing accuracy is maintained throughout; 3) All secondary control inputs converge to the same steady-state value; 4) Pinning control (only 1 DG with bus voltage access) achieves the same performance as full distributed control; 5) The method outperforms centralized control in terms of robustness to communication failures.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: Simulation results have validated the proposed method on a 4-DG test system. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: Hardware experimental validation is needed to confirm practical feasibility and demonstrate the method on a real system. [Decision] To experimentally validate, designed a hardware prototype with 2 DGs and implemented the distributed secondary control on a real-time control platform.

parameters.test_scenarios: Load step changes, communication between 2 DGs

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Experimental results are consistent with theoretical analysis and simulation results. The DC bus voltage is restored to nominal value with proper power sharing maintained. The distributed secondary control works correctly on hardware with practical communication constraints.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: Both simulation and experimental validation have been completed, confirming the effectiveness of the proposed method. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: Need to compare the proposed method with existing approaches to highlight its advantages and discuss the broader applicability. [Decision] To complete the analysis, compared the proposed method with centralized control and existing distributed methods, highlighting the advantages of feedback-based design, pinning control, and no global information requirement.

parameters.comparison_methods: Centralized PI control, feed-forward distributed methods
parameters.key_advantages: Feedback-based (more robust), no global info needed, pinning control simplification, plug-and-play capable

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Key advantages demonstrated: 1) Feedback-based design is more robust to parameter uncertainties than feed-forward approaches; 2) No global information (average current/voltage) is needed; 3) Pinning control reduces sensor requirements to just one DG; 4) The distributed architecture naturally supports plug-and-play of DGs; 5) The methodology can be extended to AC and AC/DC hybrid microgrids with appropriate modifications.

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Theoretical stability analysis with Lyapunov method establishing sufficient conditions, validated by MATLAB Simulink simulation on a 4-DG test system and experimental verification on a 2-DG hardware prototype.

Voltage Restoration Error.interpretation: DC bus voltage is restored to nominal value V* with zero steady-state error after secondary control activation.

Power Sharing Accuracy.interpretation: Proportional power sharing I_i/I_j = (R_j + k_j)/(R_i + k_i) is maintained within 3% of the designed ratio.

Control Input Consensus.interpretation: All secondary control inputs u_i converge to the same steady-state value, confirming the power sharing constraint is satisfied.

Pinning Control Effectiveness.interpretation: Only 1 DG needs DC bus voltage measurement, significantly reducing sensor requirements compared to methods requiring all DGs to have bus voltage access.

Robustness to Communication Failure.interpretation: No single-point-of-failure; the distributed architecture naturally provides fault tolerance and supports plug-and-play operation.

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The proposed distributed secondary control scheme successfully achieves both power allocation and voltage restoration in islanded DC microgrids. The feedback-based design with pinning control provides a simple, reliable, and practically feasible solution that requires minimal sensing and communication infrastructure while maintaining the power sharing accuracy established by droop control.
